fr:mainmatter.fr:p:
Consider the bounded addition function
\text {add} : \mathbb {N}_2 \times \mathbb {N}_2 \rightarrow \mathbb {N}_4
that takes two natural numbers less than or equal to 2 and returns their sum:
\text {add}(x, y) = x + y \text { for } x, y \in \mathbb {N}_2
We may define a second function
\text {add}_\text {sim} : \Sigma _2 \times \Sigma _2 \rightarrow \Sigma _4
that simulates the behavior of \text {add} by
operating on a domain of pairs of symbols \Sigma _2 \times \Sigma _2 drawn from an alphabet
\Sigma _2 = \{\ulcorner 1 \urcorner , \ulcorner 2 \urcorner , \ulcorner 3 \urcorner \}
whose members denote by assignment the natural numbers in \mathbb {N}_2;
associating with each symbol pair of the domain a symbol of the codomain
\Sigma _4 = \{\ulcorner 1 \urcorner , \ulcorner 2 \urcorner , \ulcorner 3 \urcorner , \ulcorner 4 \urcorner \}
that denotes by assignment the sum of the denoted values of the pair's symbols:
\begin {array}{ccc} (\ulcorner 1 \urcorner , \ulcorner 1 \urcorner ) & \rightarrow & \ulcorner 2 \urcorner \\ (\ulcorner 1 \urcorner , \ulcorner 2 \urcorner ) & \rightarrow & \ulcorner 3 \urcorner \\ (\ulcorner 2 \urcorner , \ulcorner 1 \urcorner ) & \rightarrow & \ulcorner 3 \urcorner \\ (\ulcorner 2 \urcorner , \ulcorner 2 \urcorner ) & \rightarrow & \ulcorner 4 \urcorner \end {array}
where the denotation is given by
\llbracket \ulcorner n \urcorner \rrbracket = n \text { for } n \in \mathbb {N}_2
and
\llbracket \ulcorner n \urcorner \rrbracket = n \text { for } n \in \mathbb {N}_4,
respectively.

It is apparent that one could not say that \text {add}_\text {sim} is adding its arguments, because the nature of the mapping is purely extensional—the relation between the domain and the codomain is here a mapping with a purely lookup operational semantics—and its domain and codomain are inhabited by symbolic expressions of the natural numbers rather than the natural numbers themselves.

There is also nothing remarkable about the choice of expressions as these are inherently arbitrary in relation to the denoted numbers. We could have just as easily chosen a different alphabet of symbols to represent the natural numbers and the simulation would still hold. The denotation of these symbols is entirely conventional in nature and, therefore, in the mind of the observer.

One could also argue that add_\text {sim} simulates the behavior of addition as such, e.g., + : \mathbb {N} \times \mathbb {N} \rightarrow \mathbb {N} as we commonly understand it, but that this simulation is partial given that it is undefined for most (expressions of the) natural numbers. This argument is plausible given the common usage in computer science (we speak of addition operators or functions in programming languages as if they were total with respect to the natural numbers or whatever the class of numerical values under consideration).
